<script>
(function (global, factory) {
    typeof exports === 'object' && typeof module !== 'undefined' ? module.exports = factory() :
    typeof define === 'function' && define.amd ? define(factory) :
    (global = global || self, global.ClipboardCopyElement = factory());
  }(this, function () { 'use strict';

    function createNode(text) {
      const node = document.createElement('pre');
      node.style.width = '1px';
      node.style.height = '1px';
      node.style.position = 'fixed';
      node.style.top = '5px';
      node.textContent = text;
      return node;
    }

    function copyNode(node) {
      if ('clipboard' in navigator) {
        // eslint-disable-next-line flowtype/no-flow-fix-me-comments
        // $FlowFixMe Clipboard is not defined in Flow yet.
        return navigator.clipboard.writeText(node.textContent);
      }

      const selection = getSelection();

      if (selection == null) {
        return Promise.reject(new Error());
      }

      selection.removeAllRanges();
      const range = document.createRange();
      range.selectNodeContents(node);
      selection.addRange(range);
      document.execCommand('copy');
      selection.removeAllRanges();
      return Promise.resolve();
    }
    function copyText(text) {
      if ('clipboard' in navigator) {
        // eslint-disable-next-line flowtype/no-flow-fix-me-comments
        // $FlowFixMe Clipboard is not defined in Flow yet.
        return navigator.clipboard.writeText(text);
      }

      const body = document.body;

      if (!body) {
        return Promise.reject(new Error());
      }

      const node = createNode(text);
      body.appendChild(node);
      copyNode(node);
      body.removeChild(node);
      return Promise.resolve();
    }

    function copy(button) {
      const id = button.getAttribute('for');
      const text = button.getAttribute('value');

      function trigger() {
        button.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('clipboard-copy', {
          bubbles: true
        }));
      }

      if (text) {
        copyText(text).then(trigger);
      } else if (id) {
        const root = 'getRootNode' in Element.prototype ? button.getRootNode() : button.ownerDocument;
        if (!(root instanceof Document || 'ShadowRoot' in window && root instanceof ShadowRoot)) return;
        const node = root.getElementById(id);
        if (node) copyTarget(node).then(trigger);
      }
    }

    function copyTarget(content) {
      if (content instanceof HTMLInputElement || content instanceof HTMLTextAreaElement) {
        return copyText(content.value);
      } else if (content instanceof HTMLAnchorElement && content.hasAttribute('href')) {
        return copyText(content.href);
      } else {
        return copyNode(content);
      }
    }

    function clicked(event) {
      const button = event.currentTarget;

      if (button instanceof HTMLElement) {
        copy(button);
      }
    }

    function keydown(event) {
      if (event.key === ' ' || event.key === 'Enter') {
        const button = event.currentTarget;

        if (button instanceof HTMLElement) {
          event.preventDefault();
          copy(button);
        }
      }
    }

    function focused(event) {
      event.currentTarget.addEventListener('keydown', keydown);
    }

    function blurred(event) {
      event.currentTarget.removeEventListener('keydown', keydown);
    }

    class ClipboardCopyElement extends HTMLElement {
      constructor() {
        super();
        this.addEventListener('click', clicked);
        this.addEventListener('focus', focused);
        this.addEventListener('blur', blurred);
      }

      connectedCallback() {
        if (!this.hasAttribute('tabindex')) {
          this.setAttribute('tabindex', '0');
        }

        if (!this.hasAttribute('role')) {
          this.setAttribute('role', 'button');
        }
      }

      get value() {
        return this.getAttribute('value') || '';
      }

      set value(text) {
        this.setAttribute('value', text);
      }

    }

    if (!window.customElements.get('clipboard-copy')) {
      window.ClipboardCopyElement = ClipboardCopyElement;
      window.customElements.define('clipboard-copy', ClipboardCopyElement);
    }

    return ClipboardCopyElement;

  }));
</script>
<script>
      document.addEventListener('clipboard-copy', function(event) {
        const notice = event.target.querySelector('.notice')
        notice.hidden = false
        setTimeout(function() {
          notice.hidden = true
        }, 1000)
      })
</script>

<script type="text/x-mathjax-config">
    init_mathjax = function() {
        if (window.MathJax) {
        // MathJax loaded
            MathJax.Hub.Config({
                TeX: {
                    equationNumbers: {
                    autoNumber: "AMS",
                    useLabelIds: true
                    }
                },
                tex2jax: {
                    inlineMath: [ ['$','$'], ["\\(","\\)"] ],
                    displayMath: [ ['$$','$$'], ["\\[","\\]"] ],
                    processEscapes: true,
                    processEnvironments: true
                },
                displayAlign: 'center',
                messageStyle: 'none',
                CommonHTML: {
                    linebreaks: {
                    automatic: true
                    }
                }
            });

            MathJax.Hub.Queue(["Typeset", MathJax.Hub]);
        }
    }
    init_mathjax();

<!-- End of mathjax configuration --><script type="module">
  document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", async () => {
    const diagrams = document.querySelectorAll(".jp-Mermaid > pre.mermaid");
    // do not load mermaidjs if not needed
    if (!diagrams.length) {
      return;
    }
    const mermaid = (await import("https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/mermaid/11.10.0/mermaid.esm.min.mjs")).default;
    const elkUrl = "https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/mermaid-layout-elk/0.1.9/mermaid-layout-elk.esm.min.mjs";
    if(elkUrl) {
      const elkLayouts = (await import(elkUrl)).default;
      mermaid.registerLayoutLoaders(elkLayouts);
    }
    const parser = new DOMParser();

    mermaid.initialize({
      maxTextSize: 100000,
      maxEdges: 100000,
      startOnLoad: false,
      fontFamily: window
        .getComputedStyle(document.body)
        .getPropertyValue("--jp-ui-font-family"),
      theme: document.querySelector("body[data-jp-theme-light='true']")
        ? "default"
        : "dark",
    });

    let _nextMermaidId = 0;

    function makeMermaidImage(svg) {
      const img = document.createElement("img");
      const doc = parser.parseFromString(svg, "image/svg+xml");
      const svgEl = doc.querySelector("svg");
      const { maxWidth } = svgEl?.style || {};
      const firstTitle = doc.querySelector("title");
      const firstDesc = doc.querySelector("desc");

      img.setAttribute("src", `data:image/svg+xml,${encodeURIComponent(svg)}`);
      if (maxWidth) {
        img.width = parseInt(maxWidth);
      }
      if (firstTitle) {
        img.setAttribute("alt", firstTitle.textContent);
      }
      if (firstDesc) {
        const caption = document.createElement("figcaption");
        caption.className = "sr-only";
        caption.textContent = firstDesc.textContent;
        return [img, caption];
      }
      return [img];
    }

    async function makeMermaidError(text) {
      let errorMessage = "";
      try {
        await mermaid.parse(text);
      } catch (err) {
        errorMessage = `${err}`;
      }

      const result = document.createElement("details");
      result.className = 'jp-RenderedMermaid-Details';
      const summary = document.createElement("summary");
      summary.className = 'jp-RenderedMermaid-Summary';
      const pre = document.createElement("pre");
      const code = document.createElement("code");
      code.innerText = text;
      pre.appendChild(code);
      summary.appendChild(pre);
      result.appendChild(summary);

      const warning = document.createElement("pre");
      warning.innerText = errorMessage;
      result.appendChild(warning);
      return [result];
    }

    async function renderOneMarmaid(src) {
      const id = `jp-mermaid-${_nextMermaidId++}`;
      const parent = src.parentNode;
      let raw = src.textContent.trim();
      const el = document.createElement("div");
      el.style.visibility = "hidden";
      document.body.appendChild(el);
      let results = null;
      let output = null;
      try {
        let { svg } = await mermaid.render(id, raw, el);
        svg = cleanMermaidSvg(svg);
        results = makeMermaidImage(svg);
        output = document.createElement("figure");
        results.map(output.appendChild, output);
      } catch (err) {
        parent.classList.add("jp-mod-warning");
        results = await makeMermaidError(raw);
        output = results[0];
      } finally {
        el.remove();
      }
      parent.classList.add("jp-RenderedMermaid");
      parent.appendChild(output);
    }


    /**
     * Post-process to ensure mermaid diagrams contain only valid SVG and XHTML.
     */
    function cleanMermaidSvg(svg) {
      svg = svg.replace(RE_VOID_ELEMENT, replaceVoidElement);
      return `${SVG_XML_HEADER}${svg}`;
    }


    /**
     * A regular expression for all void elements, which may include attributes and
     * a slash.
     *
     * @see https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Glossary/Void_element
     *
     * Of these, only `<br>` is generated by Mermaid in place of `\n`,
     * but _any_ "malformed" tag will break the SVG rendering entirely.
     */
    const RE_VOID_ELEMENT =
      /<\s*(area|base|br|col|embed|hr|img|input|link|meta|param|source|track|wbr)\s*([^>]*?)\s*>/gi;

    /**
     * Ensure a void element is closed with a slash, preserving any attributes.
     */
    function replaceVoidElement(match, tag, rest) {
      rest = rest.trim();
      if (!rest.endsWith('/')) {
        rest = `${rest} /`;
      }
      return `<${tag} ${rest}>`;
    }

<h1 id="gtsom-tutorial-mapping-mnist-handwritten-digits">GTSOM Tutorial: Mapping MNIST Handwritten Digits<a class="anchor-link" href="#gtsom-tutorial-mapping-mnist-handwritten-digits">¶</a></h1><h2 id="what-is-a-self-organising-map">What is a Self-Organising Map?<a class="anchor-link" href="#what-is-a-self-organising-map">¶</a></h2><p>A <strong>Self-Organising Map</strong> (SOM) is an unsupervised learning algorithm that projects high-dimensional data onto a low-dimensional output lattice — typically a 2D grid — while preserving the topological structure of the data. Neurons that are nearby in the output lattice learn to respond to similar inputs, so the map can be read as a kind of 'neighbourhood diagram' of the data: regions of the lattice that are close together represent data that are similar in the original high-dimensional space.</p>
<p>Unlike dimensionality reduction methods such as PCA or UMAP, the SOM output is a fixed, structured lattice whose geometry is chosen by the user. This makes the result straightforward to visualise and interpret, and allows the user to impose domain-specific structure on the output space.</p>
<p><strong>GTSOM</strong> (General Topology SOM) extends the classical SOM by allowing arbitrary output space geometries, defined by any graph structure — hexagonal grids, rectangular grids, or data-driven Delaunay triangulations built from the data itself. This tutorial demonstrates the hexagonal grid constructor (<code>GTSOM.from_grid</code>) on the MNIST handwritten digits dataset.</p>
<h3 id="what-we-will-do">What we will do<a class="anchor-link" href="#what-we-will-do">¶</a></h3><ol>
<li>Load and preprocess MNIST (10,000 images, scaled to [0,1], reduced to 50 principal components)</li>
<li>Initialise a 20×20 hexagonal GTSOM</li>
<li>Train for 200 epochs with an exponentially decaying neighbourhood bandwidth</li>
<li>Visualise the learned map: digit organisation, quantization error, and prototype images</li>
</ol>
</div>
</div>
</div>

<h2 id="2-loading-mnist">2. Loading MNIST<a class="anchor-link" href="#2-loading-mnist">¶</a></h2><p>MNIST contains 70,000 28×28 greyscale images of handwritten digits (0–9), each flattened to a 784-dimensional vector. We use the last 10,000 samples, which form the standard test split and are approximately balanced across all 10 digit classes.</p>
<p>Pixel values are scaled globally to [0, 1] before any further processing. GTSOM does not perform any internal scaling — preprocessing is the user's responsibility, and consistent scaling ensures that all input dimensions contribute equally to the nearest-neighbour search.</p>
</div>

<div class="highlight-ipynb hl-python"><pre><span></span><span class="c1"># Fetch MNIST from OpenML (downloads ~11 MB on first run, cached thereafter)</span>
<span class="n">mnist</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">fetch_openml</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s1">'mnist_784'</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">version</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="mi">1</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">as_frame</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="kc">False</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">parser</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="s1">'auto'</span><span class="p">)</span>
<span class="n">X_raw</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">mnist</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">data</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">astype</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">np</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">float32</span><span class="p">)</span>   <span class="c1"># (70000, 784)</span>
<span class="n">y_raw</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">mnist</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">target</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">astype</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="nb">int</span><span class="p">)</span>         <span class="c1"># (70000,)</span>

<span class="c1"># Use the last 10,000 samples — the standard balanced test split</span>
<span class="n">N</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="mi">10_000</span>
<span class="n">X_raw</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">X_raw</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="o">-</span><span class="n">N</span><span class="p">:]</span>
<span class="n">y</span>     <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">y_raw</span><span class="p">[</span><span class="o">-</span><span class="n">N</span><span class="p">:]</span>

<span class="nb">print</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="sa">f</span><span class="s1">'Dataset: </span><span class="si">{</span><span class="n">X_raw</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">shape</span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s1">'</span><span class="p">)</span>
<span class="nb">print</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="sa">f</span><span class="s1">'Label counts: </span><span class="si">{</span><span class="w"> </span><span class="p">{</span><span class="n">d</span><span class="p">:</span><span class="w"> </span><span class="nb">int</span><span class="p">((</span><span class="n">y</span><span class="o">==</span><span class="n">d</span><span class="p">)</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">sum</span><span class="p">())</span><span class="w"> </span><span class="k">for</span><span class="w"> </span><span class="n">d</span><span class="w"> </span><span class="ow">in</span><span class="w"> </span><span class="nb">range</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="mi">10</span><span class="p">)}</span><span class="w"> </span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s1">'</span><span class="p">)</span>

<span class="c1"># Scale globally to [0, 1]</span>
<span class="n">scaler</span>   <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">MinMaxScaler</span><span class="p">()</span>
<span class="n">X_scaled</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">scaler</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">fit_transform</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">X_raw</span><span class="p">)</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">astype</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">np</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">float32</span><span class="p">)</span>
<span class="nb">print</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="sa">f</span><span class="s1">'Scaled range: [</span><span class="si">{</span><span class="n">X_scaled</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">min</span><span class="p">()</span><span class="si">:</span><span class="s1">.3f</span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s1">, </span><span class="si">{</span><span class="n">X_scaled</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">max</span><span class="p">()</span><span class="si">:</span><span class="s1">.3f</span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s1">]'</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>
<div class="clipboard-copy-txt" id="cell-2"># Fetch MNIST from OpenML (downloads ~11 MB on first run, cached thereafter)
mnist = fetch_openml('mnist_784', version=1, as_frame=False, parser='auto')
X_raw = mnist.data.astype(np.float32)   # (70000, 784)
y_raw = mnist.target.astype(int)         # (70000,)

# Use the last 10,000 samples — the standard balanced test split
N = 10_000
X_raw = X_raw[-N:]
y     = y_raw[-N:]

print(f'Dataset: {X_raw.shape}')
print(f'Label counts: { {d: int((y==d).sum()) for d in range(10)} }')

# Scale globally to [0, 1]
scaler   = MinMaxScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_raw).astype(np.float32)
print(f'Scaled range: [{X_scaled.min():.3f}, {X_scaled.max():.3f}]')</div>

<h2 id="3-dimensionality-reduction-via-pca">3. Dimensionality Reduction via PCA<a class="anchor-link" href="#3-dimensionality-reduction-via-pca">¶</a></h2><p>With d=784 input dimensions, computing nearest-neighbour distances for each data point at every training epoch would be expensive and dominated by uninformative dimensions (the blank border pixels common to all images). We reduce to 50 principal components, which captures approximately 85% of the total variance while dramatically reducing computation time.</p>
<p>This PCA is a preprocessing step applied to the raw pixel data. The SOM then operates in this 50-dimensional PCA score space.</p>
</div>
</div>
</div>
</div><div class="jp-Cell jp-CodeCell jp-Notebook-cell">

<div class="highlight-ipynb hl-python"><pre><span></span><span class="n">N_PCS</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="mi">50</span>
<span class="n">pca</span>   <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">PCA</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">n_components</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="n">N_PCS</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">svd_solver</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="s1">'randomized'</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">random_state</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="mi">42</span><span class="p">)</span>
<span class="n">X</span>     <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">pca</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">fit_transform</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">X_scaled</span><span class="p">)</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">astype</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">np</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">float32</span><span class="p">)</span>
<span class="nb">print</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="sa">f</span><span class="s1">'Reduced: </span><span class="si">{</span><span class="n">X_scaled</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">shape</span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s1"> -> </span><span class="si">{</span><span class="n">X</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">shape</span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s1">'</span><span class="p">)</span>
<span class="nb">print</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="sa">f</span><span class="s1">'Variance explained by </span><span class="si">{</span><span class="n">N_PCS</span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s1"> PCs: </span><span class="si">{</span><span class="n">pca</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">explained_variance_ratio_</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">sum</span><span class="p">()</span><span class="o">*</span><span class="mi">100</span><span class="si">:</span><span class="s1">.1f</span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s1">%'</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>
<div class="clipboard-copy-txt" id="cell-3">N_PCS = 50
pca   = PCA(n_components=N_PCS, svd_solver='randomized', random_state=42)
X     = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled).astype(np.float32)
print(f'Reduced: {X_scaled.shape} -> {X.shape}')
print(f'Variance explained by {N_PCS} PCs: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_.sum()*100:.1f}%')</div>

<span class="n">ax</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">legend</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">fontsize</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="mi">8</span><span class="p">)</span>
<span class="n">ax</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">grid</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="kc">True</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">alpha</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="mf">0.3</span><span class="p">)</span>

<div class="clipboard-copy-txt" id="cell-4"># Scree plot: cumulative variance explained
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3))
cumvar = np.cumsum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_) * 100
ax.plot(range(1, N_PCS + 1), cumvar, 'o-', ms=3, lw=1.5, color='steelblue')
ax.axhline(85, color='dimgrey', lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='85% variance')
ax.axhline(95, color='coral',   lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='95% variance')
ax.set_xlabel('Number of principal components')
ax.set_ylabel('Cumulative variance explained (%)')
ax.set_title('MNIST PCA — Cumulative Variance Explained')
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()</div>

<div class="jp-Cell jp-MarkdownCell jp-Notebook-cell" id="cell-id=93b02a31">
<div class="jp-Cell-inputWrapper" tabindex="0">
<div class="jp-Collapser jp-InputCollapser jp-Cell-inputCollapser">

<div class="jp-InputArea jp-Cell-inputArea"><div class="jp-InputPrompt jp-InputArea-prompt">

<h2 id="4-choosing-the-output-lattice">4. Choosing the Output Lattice<a class="anchor-link" href="#4-choosing-the-output-lattice">¶</a></h2><h3 id="lattice-size">Lattice size<a class="anchor-link" href="#lattice-size">¶</a></h3><p>A common rule of thumb for SOM size is M ≈ 5√N neurons, where N is the number of training samples. For N=10,000 this gives M ≈ 500, suggesting a grid of roughly 22×22. We use a <strong>20×20 grid (M=400)</strong>, which is slightly more conservative but trains faster and remains visually interpretable. With 400 neurons and 10,000 data points, each neuron will serve on average 25 data points in its receptive field.</p>
<p>Too few neurons: multiple digit classes are forced to share prototypes, blurring the map. Too many neurons: training is slow and many neurons end up with empty receptive fields.</p>
<h3 id="hexagonal-vs-rectangular-grid">Hexagonal vs rectangular grid<a class="anchor-link" href="#hexagonal-vs-rectangular-grid">¶</a></h3><p>We use a hexagonal grid (<code>coord_init='hex'</code>) rather than rectangular. In a hex grid every interior neuron has exactly 6 equidistant neighbours, giving a more uniform neighbourhood structure than a rectangular grid where diagonal neighbours are further away than axis-aligned ones. This tends to produce smoother, more isotropic maps.</p>
<h3 id="weight-initialisation">Weight initialisation<a class="anchor-link" href="#weight-initialisation">¶</a></h3><p><code>W_init='random'</code> initialises each prototype by sampling a random data point from X. This gives a starting configuration that is well-distributed within the data cloud. The SOM training then moves prototypes from these initial positions into the regions of the data space they will ultimately represent.</p>
</div>
</div>
</div>
</div>
<div class="jp-Cell jp-MarkdownCell jp-Notebook-cell" id="cell-id=2d1f21ec">
<div class="jp-Cell-inputWrapper" tabindex="0">

</div>
<div class="jp-InputArea jp-Cell-inputArea"><div class="jp-InputPrompt jp-InputArea-prompt">
</div><div class="jp-RenderedHTMLCommon jp-RenderedMarkdown jp-MarkdownOutput" data-mime-type="text/markdown">
<h2 id="5-initialising-the-gtsom">5. Initialising the GTSOM<a class="anchor-link" href="#5-initialising-the-gtsom">¶</a></h2>
</div>
</div>
</div>
</div><div class="jp-Cell jp-CodeCell jp-Notebook-cell">
<div class="jp-Cell jp-CodeCell jp-Notebook-cell" id="cell-id=c2abb95a">
<div class="jp-Cell-inputWrapper" tabindex="0">
<div class="jp-Collapser jp-InputCollapser jp-Cell-inputCollapser">
</div>
<div class="jp-InputArea jp-Cell-inputArea">
<div class="jp-InputPrompt jp-InputArea-prompt">In [5]:</div><div class="jp-CodeMirrorEditor jp-Editor jp-InputArea-editor" data-type="inline">
<div class="CodeMirror cm-s-jupyter">
<div class="zeroclipboard-container">
<clipboard-copy ,="" aria-label="Copy to Clipboard" for="cell-5">
<div>
<span class="notice" hidden="">Copied!</span>
<svg aria-hidden="true" class="clipboard-copy-icon" data-view-component="true" height="20" version="1.1" viewbox="0 0 16 16" width="20">
<path d="M0 6.75C0 5.784.784 5 1.75 5h1.5a.75.75 0 010 1.5h-1.5a.25.25 0 00-.25.25v7.5c0 .138.112.25.25.25h7.5a.25.25 0 00.25-.25v-1.5a.75.75 0 011.5 0v1.5A1.75 1.75 0 019.25 16h-7.5A1.75 1.75 0 010 14.25v-7.5z" fill="currentColor" fill-rule="evenodd"></path>
<path d="M5 1.75C5 .784 5.784 0 6.75 0h7.5C15.216 0 16 .784 16 1.75v7.5A1.75 1.75 0 0114.25 11h-7.5A1.75 1.75 0 015 9.25v-7.5zm1.75-.25a.25.25 0 00-.25.25v7.5c0 .138.112.25.25.25h7.5a.25.25 0 00.25-.25v-7.5a.25.25 0 00-.25-.25h-7.5z" fill="currentColor" fill-rule="evenodd"></path>
</svg>

<div class="highlight-ipynb hl-python"><pre><span></span><span class="n">SEED</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="mi">42</span>

<span class="n">som</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="n">GTSOM</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">from_grid</span><span class="p">(</span>
    <span class="n">X</span><span class="p">,</span>
    <span class="n">shape</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="mi">20</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="mi">20</span><span class="p">),</span>
    <span class="n">coord_init</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="s1">'hex'</span><span class="p">,</span>
    <span class="n">W_init</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="s1">'random'</span><span class="p">,</span>
    <span class="n">random_state</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="n">SEED</span><span class="p">,</span>
    <span class="n">labels</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="n">y</span><span class="p">,</span>
<span class="p">)</span>
<span class="nb">print</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">som</span><span class="p">)</span>
<span class="nb">print</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="sa">f</span><span class="s1">'Prototypes with empty receptive fields at init: '</span>
      <span class="sa">f</span><span class="s1">'</span><span class="si">{</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">som</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">recaller</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">RFSize</span><span class="w"> </span><span class="o">==</span><span class="w"> </span><span class="mi">0</span><span class="p">)</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">sum</span><span class="p">()</span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s1"> / </span><span class="si">{</span><span class="n">som</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">M</span><span class="si">}</span><span class="s1">'</span><span class="p">)</span>
</pre></div>
<div class="clipboard-copy-txt" id="cell-5">SEED = 42

som = GTSOM.from_grid(
    X,
    shape=(20, 20),
    coord_init='hex',
    W_init='random',
    random_state=SEED,
    labels=y,
)
print(som)
print(f'Prototypes with empty receptive fields at init: '
      f'{(som.recaller.RFSize == 0).sum()} / {som.M}')</div>

<div class="jp-Cell jp-MarkdownCell jp-Notebook-cell" id="cell-id=751fdfc9">
<div class="jp-Cell-inputWrapper" tabindex="0">

</div>
<div class="jp-InputArea jp-Cell-inputArea"><div class="jp-InputPrompt jp-InputArea-prompt">

<h2 id="6-the-learning-schedule">6. The Learning Schedule<a class="anchor-link" href="#6-the-learning-schedule">¶</a></h2><p>Before training, we configure the <strong>neighbourhood bandwidth schedule</strong> — the parameter ρ (rho) that controls how widely each prototype update propagates across the lattice.</p>
<h3 id="how-the-som-update-rule-works">How the SOM update rule works<a class="anchor-link" href="#how-the-som-update-rule-works">¶</a></h3><p>At each training epoch, every data point is assigned to its nearest prototype (the Best Matching Unit, or BMU). The BMU and its neighbours are then pulled toward the data points assigned to the BMU. The neighbourhood influence of prototype j on prototype b's update is determined by a kernel weight h(dist(b,j), ρ) that falls off with geodesic distance on the lattice. A large ρ means a wide neighbourhood: many prototypes move together, producing coarse global organisation. A small ρ means only immediate neighbours are affected, producing fine local refinement.</p>
<h3 id="parameter-choices">Parameter choices<a class="anchor-link" href="#parameter-choices">¶</a></h3><p><strong><code>rho_0 = 10.0</code> — initial bandwidth.</strong> The 20×20 hex lattice has a diameter of approximately 20 geodesic hops. Starting with ρ=10 means each prototype's neighbourhood initially spans roughly half the lattice, ensuring the first training epochs establish global organisation rather than local rearrangement.</p>
<p><strong><code>rho_f = 1.0</code> — final bandwidth.</strong> At ρ=1, only immediately adjacent neurons (6 neighbours in a hex grid) influence each other's updates. This is the standard SOM minimum: the map continues to refine locally but the global topology is fixed.</p>
<p><strong><code>halflife_epochs = 100</code>.</strong> The bandwidth decays exponentially from ρ₀ to ρ_f; <code>halflife_epochs</code> sets the half-life of this decay (the geometric midpoint between ρ₀ and ρ_f is reached at <code>halflife_epochs</code>, and ρ_f is reached at <code>2 × halflife_epochs</code>). With 200 total epochs and <code>halflife_epochs=100</code>, the bandwidth is actively decaying throughout the first half of training and at its floor for the second half — a natural two-phase structure: global organisation followed by local refinement.</p>
<p><strong><code>n_jobs=-1</code></strong> enables parallel prototype updates using all available CPU cores via a numba JIT-compiled kernel, significantly accelerating training on large datasets.</p>
</div>
</div>
</div>
</div><div class="jp-Cell jp-CodeCell jp-Notebook-cell">
<div class="jp-Cell jp-CodeCell jp-Notebook-cell" id="cell-id=3297ca33">
<div class="jp-Cell-inputWrapper" tabindex="0">
<div class="jp-Collapser jp-InputCollapser jp-Cell-inputCollapser">
</div>
<div class="jp-InputArea jp-Cell-inputArea">

<span class="n">ax</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">set_xlabel</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s1">'Epoch'</span><span class="p">)</span>

<span class="n">ax</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">set_title</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="s1">'Exponential bandwidth decay schedule'</span><span class="p">)</span>
<span class="n">ax</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">legend</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="n">fontsize</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="mi">8</span><span class="p">)</span>
<span class="n">ax</span><span class="o">.</span><span class="n">grid</span><span class="p">(</span><span class="kc">True</span><span class="p">,</span> <span class="n">alpha</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="mf">0.3</span><span class="p">)</span>

</pre></div>

<h2 id="7-training">7. Training<a class="anchor-link" href="#7-training">¶</a></h2><p>We train for 200 epochs. A snapshot of the SOM state is captured every 25 epochs and stored in <code>learn_history_</code> for retrospective inspection. The verbose output below shows the epoch number, current bandwidth ρ, global mean quantization error (MQE), and the proportion of data points whose nearest prototype changed since the previous epoch (delBMU).</p>
</div>

<h2 id="8-learning-curves">8. Learning Curves<a class="anchor-link" href="#8-learning-curves">¶</a></h2><p>Two quantities are monitored at every epoch:</p>
<p><strong>Mean Quantization Error (MQE)</strong> is the average Euclidean distance from each data point to its nearest prototype. It measures how well the prototypes represent the data — lower is better. MQE generally decreases monotonically as training progresses.</p>
<p><strong>delBMU</strong> is the proportion of data points whose nearest prototype (BMU) changed from the previous epoch. It measures how much the map is still reorganising — a value near 0 indicates convergence. The periodic spikes visible in the delBMU curve reflect <em>neighbourhood shell collapses</em>: as ρ decays, the kernel weight for prototypes at distance d geodesic hops drops below a minimum threshold, effectively removing that shell from the neighbourhood. Each such collapse triggers a brief burst of BMU reassignments before the map re-settles.</p>

<div class="clipboard-copy-txt" id="cell-9">ages_arr   = np.array([s['age']    for s in som.learn_history_])
mqes_arr   = np.array([s['mqe']    for s in som.learn_history_])
delbmu_arr = np.array([s['delBMU'] for s in som.learn_history_])

fig, (ax_mqe, ax_del) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4))

ax_mqe.plot(ages_arr, mqes_arr, 'o-', lw=1.5, ms=3, color='steelblue')
ax_mqe.axvline(0, color='dimgrey', lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='Init (age=0)')
ax_mqe.set_xlabel('Age (epochs)')
ax_mqe.set_ylabel('Global MQE')
ax_mqe.set_title('SOM Learning\nGlobal MQE')
ax_mqe.legend(fontsize=8)
ax_mqe.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

ax_del.plot(ages_arr, delbmu_arr, 'o-', lw=1.5, ms=3, color='darkorange')
ax_del.axvline(0, color='dimgrey', lw=0.8, linestyle='--', label='Init (age=0)')
ax_del.set_xlabel('Age (epochs)')
ax_del.set_ylabel('delBMU')
ax_del.set_title('SOM Learning\nProportion of changed BMUs')
ax_del.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.05)
ax_del.legend(fontsize=8)
ax_del.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()</div>

<h2 id="9-lattice-organisation-over-time">9. Lattice Organisation Over Time<a class="anchor-link" href="#9-lattice-organisation-over-time">¶</a></h2><p>The following four panels show the SOM lattice coloured by the plurality digit label at each neuron, captured at ages 0, 50, 100, and 200. At age 0 the labels are randomly distributed. By age 50 a coarse global structure is already visible — similar digits are beginning to cluster spatially. The map continues to sharpen through the remaining epochs as the neighbourhood bandwidth narrows and prototypes refine their local territories.</p>
</div>

<div class="clipboard-copy-txt" id="cell-10">import io
from PIL import Image as PILImage

target_ages = [0, 50, 100, 200]
snap_by_age = {s['age']: s for s in som.learn_history_ if s['fig'] is not None}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 12))
fig.suptitle('GTSOM — MNIST lattice organisation over training',
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', y=1.01)

for ax, age in zip(axes.flat, target_ages):
    snap_fig = snap_by_age[age]['fig']  # plotnine ggplot object
    # Save ggplot to an in-memory PNG, then display with imshow
    buf = io.BytesIO()
    snap_fig.save(buf, format='png', dpi=100, width=6, height=5)
    buf.seek(0)
    img = PILImage.open(buf)
    ax.imshow(img)
    ax.axis('off')
    ax.set_title(f'Age = {age}', fontsize=12, pad=6)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
</div>

<h2 id="10-final-learned-map">10. Final Learned Map<a class="anchor-link" href="#10-final-learned-map">¶</a></h2><h3 id="digit-organisation">Digit organisation<a class="anchor-link" href="#digit-organisation">¶</a></h3><p>The map below shows the final trained SOM coloured by the plurality digit label at each neuron. The SOM has learned a spatially coherent organisation of the digit classes — similar-looking digits occupy neighbouring regions of the lattice.</p>
<p>The topology reflects the visual similarity structure of the digits: curved digits (0, 6, 8, 9) tend to cluster together, as do straight-stroked digits (1, 4, 7). Digits with high intra-class variability (notably 5, which can resemble 3, 6, or 8 depending on the writer) may appear scattered or occupy boundary regions between other digit clusters.</p>
</div>
</div>
</div>

<h3 id="quantization-error-and-receptive-field-size">Quantization error and receptive field size<a class="anchor-link" href="#quantization-error-and-receptive-field-size">¶</a></h3><p>Two further diagnostic views of the trained map:</p>
<p><strong>Mean Quantization Error per neuron</strong> (<code>color_by='mqe'</code>) shows where the map fits the data well (low MQE, dark) and where prototypes are further from their assigned data points (high MQE, bright). High-MQE regions often correspond to class boundaries where a single prototype must represent data from multiple nearby clusters.</p>
<p><strong>Receptive field size</strong> (<code>color_by='rfsize'</code>) shows how many data points are assigned to each neuron. A uniform distribution across neurons is ideal; neurons with very large receptive fields are 'overloaded' and may benefit from a larger lattice.</p>
</div>
</div>
</div>
</div><div class="jp-Cell jp-CodeCell jp-Notebook-cell jp-mod-noOutputs">

<h2 id="11-prototype-images">11. Prototype Images<a class="anchor-link" href="#11-prototype-images">¶</a></h2><p>Each neuron's weight vector W[j] is a point in 50-dimensional PCA score space. By applying the inverse PCA transform, we can reconstruct the 28×28 pixel image that each neuron has learned to represent — the 'template' for its region of the digit space. Arranging these prototype images on the hex lattice produces a visual summary of the entire learned map.</p>
<p>Prototypes in the interior of a digit cluster should resemble that digit clearly; prototypes at boundaries between clusters will show blended or ambiguous forms — a natural consequence of the SOM's smooth topographic mapping.</p>
</div>
</div>
</div>
</div><div class="jp-Cell jp-CodeCell jp-Notebook-cell">

<div class="clipboard-copy-txt" id="cell-13"># Back-project W from PCA score space to pixel space
W_pixels = pca.inverse_transform(som.W)          # (M, 784)
W_pixels = scaler.inverse_transform(W_pixels)    # undo [0,1] scaling
W_pixels = np.clip(W_pixels, 0, 255).astype(np.uint8)

coords  = som.embed.coords   # (M, 2) hex lattice positions
tile    = 28
x_min, x_max = coords[:, 0].min(), coords[:, 0].max()
y_min, y_max = coords[:, 1].min(), coords[:, 1].max()
x_range = x_max - x_min
y_range = y_max - y_min

canvas_w = int(20 * tile * 1.15)
canvas_h = int(20 * tile * 1.25)
canvas   = np.ones((canvas_h, canvas_w), dtype=np.uint8) * 255

for m in range(som.M):
    px = int((coords[m, 0] - x_min) / x_range * (canvas_w - tile))
    py = int((coords[m, 1] - y_min) / y_range * (canvas_h - tile))
    py = canvas_h - tile - py   # flip y
    py = max(0, min(py, canvas_h - tile))
    px = max(0, min(px, canvas_w - tile))
    canvas[py:py+tile, px:px+tile] = W_pixels[m].reshape(28, 28)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 10))
ax.imshow(canvas, cmap='gray_r', vmin=0, vmax=255)
ax.axis('off')
ax.set_title(
    'GTSOM — MNIST Prototype Images\n'
    '(each neuron\'s weight vector back-projected to pixel space)',
    fontsize=11,
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()</div>

<h2 id="12-summary">12. Summary<a class="anchor-link" href="#12-summary">¶</a></h2><p>In this tutorial we trained a 20×20 hexagonal GTSOM on 10,000 MNIST digit images reduced to 50 principal components. The trained map shows clear topographic organisation: digit classes occupy contiguous regions of the lattice, and the spatial relationships between regions reflect the visual similarity of the digits.</p>
<p>Key parameters and their roles:</p>
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Parameter</th>
<th>Value</th>
<th>Role</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Grid shape</td>
<td>20×20 hex</td>
<td>M=400 neurons; balanced coverage for N=10,000</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><code>W_init</code></td>
<td><code>'random'</code></td>
<td>Prototypes initialised from random data points</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><code>rho_0</code></td>
<td>10.0</td>
<td>Initial bandwidth ≈ half the lattice diameter</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><code>rho_f</code></td>
<td>1.0</td>
<td>Final bandwidth = single-neighbour influence</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><code>halflife_epochs</code></td>
<td>100</td>
<td>Bandwidth half-life; ρ_f reached at epoch 200</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><code>n_epochs</code></td>
<td>200</td>
<td>Total training epochs</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<h3 id="next-steps">Next steps<a class="anchor-link" href="#next-steps">¶</a></h3><ul>
<li><strong>Data-driven topology</strong>: <code>GTSOM.from_data</code> builds the output lattice via Delaunay triangulation of prototype positions in PCA or Laplacian Eigenmap space, allowing the topology to adapt to the data rather than being fixed in advance.</li>
<li><strong>Larger grids</strong>: For exploratory analysis of large datasets, a 30×30 or 40×40 grid provides finer spatial resolution at the cost of longer training time.</li>
<li><strong>Parallelism</strong>: <code>n_jobs</code> in <code>compile()</code> controls the number of CPU threads used for prototype updates. On multi-core machines, <code>n_jobs=-1</code> (all cores) can reduce training time substantially for large N and d.</li>
</ul>
</div>
